title: React useState
tldr: useState is the primary hook for adding state to functional components.
const [state, setState] = useState(initialValue) returns current state and a setter.
Calling the setter triggers a re-render of the component.
State updates are asynchronous and batched in React 18 for performance.
laymanDefinition: useState is like a scoreboard with a remote control. The scoreboard displays a number (state value). The remote has buttons (setter) to change the number. When you press a button, the scoreboard updates to show the new number, and everyone watching (the UI) sees the change. You can also press the button with a note saying 'make it one more than before' (functional update) to avoid mistakes.

deepDive.heading: Basic Usage
deepDive.text: const [count, setCount] = useState(0). useState takes the initial value and returns an array. Destructure it to get the current value and the updater function. The initial value is only used on the first render.

deepDive.heading: Functional Updates
deepDive.text: setCount(prev => prev + 1). When the new state depends on the previous state, use the functional form. This avoids bugs with stale closures when multiple updates happen in the same render cycle.

deepDive.heading: Lazy Initial State
deepDive.text: useState(() => expensiveComputation()). If the initial state is expensive to compute, pass a function instead of a value. The function only runs once (on initial render), not on every re-render.

deepDive.heading: State Update Batching
deepDive.text: React 18 batches all state updates (event handlers, timeouts, promises). Multiple setState calls in the same event produce a single re-render. Use functional updates when reading state within the batch.

interviewAnswer: useState is the fundamental hook for functional component state. It returns [currentValue, setterFunction]. The setter triggers a re-render. Use functional updates (prev => prev + 1) when new state depends on old state. Use lazy initialization for expensive computations. State updates are async and batched. Never mutate state directly - always use the setter.

interviewQuestions.question: What does useState return?
interviewQuestions.answer: An array [currentValue, setterFunction]. const [count, setCount] = useState(0).

interviewQuestions.question: What happens when you call the setter?
interviewQuestions.answer: The component re-renders with the new state value. If the same value (by reference), React may skip the re-render.

interviewQuestions.question: What is a functional update?
interviewQuestions.answer: setCount(prev => prev + 1). Pass a function instead of a value. Safe when new state depends on old state.

interviewQuestions.question: When to use lazy initialization?
interviewQuestions.answer: When initial state requires expensive computation: useState(() => computeExpensiveValue()). Runs only once.

interviewQuestions.question: Are state updates synchronous?
interviewQuestions.answer: No - they are async and batched. Multiple setState calls trigger a single re-render.

interviewQuestions.question: What happens if you mutate state directly?
interviewQuestions.answer: React doesn't detect the change (reference hasn't changed). No re-render occurs. Always use the setter.

interviewQuestions.question: Can useState store objects and arrays?
interviewQuestions.answer: Yes, but always replace them immutably: setUser({ ...user, name: 'New' }). Never mutate the object directly.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the bail out condition?
interviewQuestions.answer: If the setter is called with the same value (Object.is comparison), React skips re-rendering the children.

codeExamples.title: Counter with useState Patterns
codeExamples.description: Functional updates (prev => prev + 1) are safe with batching. Object state requires immutable spread. Lazy init runs once.

codeExamples.title: State with Previous Value Toggle
codeExamples.description: Boolean state uses functional toggle: setIsOn(prev => !prev). Reset uses direct setter call.

mcqQuestions.question: Are state updates synchronous?
options: No, they are async and batched
